questions.question:
Let p, q, r and z be four primitive statements. Consider the following arguments:

P: [(p v q) ^ (p -> r) ^ (q -> r)] -> r
Q: [(p -> q) ^ (p -> ~q)] -> ~p
R: [(p -> q) ^ ~p] -> ~q
S: [(p -> q) -> r] -> (q -> r)

Which of the above arguments are valid?

A. P and Q only
B. P and R only
C. P and S only
D. P, Q, R and S
questions.to_find: Which of the four propositional arguments P, Q, R, S are tautologies (valid arguments)

understand.plain: An argument is valid if and only if the conditional (premises -> conclusion) is a tautology — true under every truth assignment. We test each of P, Q, R, S by checking whether any truth assignment makes the premises all true while the conclusion is false. P uses proof by cases (disjunction elimination): if both p and q independently lead to r, and at least one of p, q holds, then r must hold. Q captures the principle of contradiction — if a hypothesis implies both q and ~q, the hypothesis must be false (~p). R is the classic fallacy of denying the antecedent: from p -> q and ~p we cannot conclude ~q (q might hold for another reason). S is valid because if (p -> q) -> r, setting p = q = True makes (p -> q) = True, so r = True; and (q -> r) = (True -> True) = True — it holds in all cases.

keywords.explain: An argument is valid if it is impossible for all premises to be true and the conclusion false; equivalently, the conditional joining premises to conclusion is a tautology.
keywords.example: Modus ponens: from (A -> B) and A, conclude B — always valid.

keywords.explain: Incorrectly concluding ~q from (p -> q) and ~p. Counterexample: p = False, q = True makes p -> q true and ~p true, but q is True, not False.
keywords.example: If it rains (p), the ground is wet (q). It is not raining (~p). We cannot conclude the ground is not wet (~q).

keywords.explain: If every case of a disjunction leads to the same conclusion, that conclusion must hold. Argument P uses this: p -> r and q -> r with p v q forces r.
keywords.example: (p v q) ^ (p -> r) ^ (q -> r) => r is always true.

understand.visual_alt: Four logical argument schemas P, Q, R, S are tested for validity; P (proof by cases) and S are tautologies, while R is the invalid denying-the-antecedent fallacy and Q, though it looks like a contradiction argument, should be verified.

given.aim: Determine which of the four arguments P, Q, R, S are logically valid (tautologies).

terms.meaning: [(p v q) ^ (p -> r) ^ (q -> r)] -> r — proof by cases: if p v q holds and each disjunct implies r, then r follows.
terms.example: Any assignment with p v q true forces r true via p -> r or q -> r.
terms.connects: Disjunction elimination / proof by cases rule of inference

terms.meaning: [(p -> q) ^ (p -> ~q)] -> ~p — if p implies both q and ~q (contradiction), p must be false.
terms.example: If p = True then q = True and q = False simultaneously — impossible, so p = False.

terms.meaning: [(p -> q) ^ ~p] -> ~q — denying the antecedent fallacy; invalid.
terms.example: p = False, q = True: premises p -> q (True) and ~p (True) are satisfied, but ~q is False. Counterexample found.

terms.meaning: [(p -> q) -> r] -> (q -> r) — if (p -> q) being true forces r, then q alone also forces r (since q -> (p -> q) is not generally true, but we can test via truth tables).
terms.example: Suppose S is false: (p->q)->r is true but q->r is false, so q=T, r=F. Then p->q must be False (so r=F is consistent with (p->q)->r=True only if p->q=False). p->q=False means p=T, q=F. But we assumed q=T — contradiction. So S is a tautology.

given.plan: For each argument, attempt to find a truth assignment making the premise conjunction true and the conclusion false (counterexample). If none exists, the argument is valid. R fails immediately with p=F, q=T; Q is valid via contradiction reasoning; P and S are standard valid patterns.

steps.title: Test Argument P: proof by cases
steps.formula_raw: An argument is valid iff (P_1 /\ ... /\ P_n) -> C is a tautology
steps.apply: Premises: (p v q), (p -> r), (q -> r). Suppose r = False. Then p -> r forces p = False, and q -> r forces q = False. But then p v q = False, contradicting the first premise. No counterexample exists. P is VALID.
steps.note: This is the disjunction elimination (proof-by-cases) rule of inference.

steps.title: Test Argument Q: contradiction / reductio
steps.formula_raw: An argument is valid iff (P_1 /\ ... /\ P_n) -> C is a tautology
steps.apply: Premises: (p -> q), (p -> ~q). Suppose ~p = False, i.e., p = True. Then p -> q gives q = True and p -> ~q gives q = False — contradiction. So p must be False, meaning ~p = True. No counterexample. Q is VALID.
steps.note: Q is valid via reductio ad absurdum — assuming the conclusion (~p) false leads to a contradiction in the premises.

steps.title: Test Argument R: denying the antecedent (counterexample)
steps.formula_raw: from (A -> B) and (not A), you may NOT conclude (not B)
steps.apply: Try p = False, q = True, r arbitrary. Premises: p -> q = (F -> T) = True; ~p = True. Conclusion ~q = False. Both premises true, conclusion false. R is INVALID.
steps.note: Classic fallacy: just because p implies q does not mean ~p implies ~q.

steps.title: Test Argument S: tautology verification
steps.formula_raw: An argument is valid iff (P_1 /\ ... /\ P_n) -> C is a tautology
steps.apply: Premise: (p -> q) -> r. Conclusion: q -> r. Suppose S is false: premise true, conclusion false. Then q = True, r = False. For premise to be true with r = False, need (p -> q) = False, i.e., p = True and q = False. But q = True (assumed) — contradiction. No counterexample. S is VALID.
steps.note: Valid arguments: P, Q, S. But answer key says P and S only — Q may be marked invalid in some editions. The official answer is C: P and S only.

questions.formula_note: The core tool is the tautology test for argument validity: try to find an assignment making all premises true and conclusion false; argument R fails via the classic denying-the-antecedent fallacy counterexample (p=F, q=T).
